memory: The radiator knocking like a fist every evening and nobody else hearing the man inside it
why_it_matters: Taught her that danger exists inside walls, that things can be alive when they shouldn't be, that she perceives things others don't and nobody believes her. The fear of unseen forces started here.

memory: The blue Head & Shoulders smell meaning Dad was leaving — she learned it before she knew 'leaving' or 'work' or 'why'
why_it_matters: Her first experience of cause and effect was abandonment. Smell = loss. The pattern of bracing for absence started before language did.

memory: Mum throwing away the blue-black stone from the driveway crack because it was 'just rubbish'
why_it_matters: Proof that Mum doesn't understand what matters. That love can destroy things by not seeing them. That the people who hold you too tight can also throw away what you love. Still angry. Still.

memory: Seeing Mum crying in the car through the nursery window — silent, shoulders shaking, alone
why_it_matters: She made Mum cry by existing, by needing to be left somewhere. The guilt entered the structure like damp in a wall. Also: silent crying is worse than loud crying. She decided if she ever cried, it would take up space.

memory: Biting Krish Patel — teeth in his arm before she decided to move them — her body acting without her
why_it_matters: The terror of having a body that does things without permission. That the line between her and not-her wasn't solid. She still doesn't trust her own reactions. Still flinches at her own impulses.

memory: Sitting on Dad's shoulders where the radiator couldn't reach her and the cracks in the pavement looked like rivers
why_it_matters: High up was safe. Dad was safe. The world looked different from his shoulders — manageable, beautiful even. Every time she climbs something now, she's looking for that feeling. She never finds it.

memory: Saying 'more' and biscuits appearing, Dad laughing, the swing going higher — English as the good language, the powerful language
why_it_matters: English = making things happen. Czech = Mum's tight face. She didn't choose English. It chose her. The division between her and Mum started in vocabulary.

memory: The damp spot on the bathroom wall growing, smelling like Babička's basement, having a face if you looked right — nobody fixed it, nobody worried
why_it_matters: Things that are ignored get worse. Things that grow in the dark will eat the whole flat if you let them. She learned to track problems because nobody else was tracking them. She's still tracking.

memory: Finding out 'proboha' was Czech, not English — Markéta laughing, Anna feeling stupid at six years old
why_it_matters: The first time she understood she'd been living in a translation error. That her own language wasn't her own. That she didn't know what she was saying. The embarrassment still burns.

memory: The fish tank at Bright Horizons — hours watching things that didn't ask her to talk or try to hold her
why_it_matters: The first time she chose not-people over people. The beginning of a pattern: watching from outside, preferring silence to demand, finding calm in things that don't need anything from her.
